steps.title: Track the rule change
steps.content: The earlier CE rule blocked categorical exclusions for actions that had significant effects either individually or cumulatively. The revised 2020 rule focused on actions that normally do not have a significant effect. The important change is the loss of explicit attention to cumulative effects across multiple actions.

steps.title: Identify the risk
steps.content: Environmentalists would be concerned if agencies could approve quick reviews for projects that look minor one by one but become harmful when grouped together. That concern follows directly from changing a rule that considered cumulative impact into one that asks whether an action normally has a significant environmental effect.

steps.title: Reject reversed choices
steps.content: Choice A invents a highway-building incentive not stated in the text. Choice B says more exclusions would result in more reviews and a slower process, which reverses the purpose of CEs. Choice C reverses the timeline by claiming the old rule allowed expedited reviews for significant rare effects. Choice D captures the cumulative-effect concern. The answer is D.
